payload.text:
Add to the website at the bottom of the page, developed by Dev Lido.

بالاسفل

**Designing the Footer**

I'm focusing on the footer implementation, planning to include "Developed by Dev Lido" and the Arabic translation "تم التطوير بواسطة ديف ليدو" at the very bottom. Thinking about the best way to integrate this cleanly into the current design, considering accessibility and responsive behavior.


**Evaluating Layout Placement**

I've been examining the current layout structure to determine the ideal location for the footer. Currently, `App.tsx` manages the core layout with a sidebar and main content area. Thinking that integrating the footer within the `main` tag seems logical, or at the very bottom overall. Since the main area uses `overflow-y-auto`, I'm evaluating how this will impact the footer's visibility and responsiveness. I want a solution that keeps the footer consistently visible regardless of scroll position.


**Positioning the Footer**

I'm now integrating the footer into `App.tsx`, placing it at the very end of the `main` content section. This ensures it appears at the bottom of the visible content. After some consideration, I decided against including it in `Sidebar.tsx`, favoring a global footer within the main scrolling area for consistency. I am now working on implementing "Developed by Dev Lido" in Arabic "تم التطوير بواسطة ديف ليدو" at the bottom of the page.


**Adjusting the Styling**

I'm now integrating the footer into `App.tsx`, placing it at the very end of the `main` content section. This ensures it appears at the bottom of the visible content. After some consideration, I decided against including it in `Sidebar.tsx`, favoring a global footer within the main scrolling area for consistency. I am now working on implementing "Developed by Dev Lido" in Arabic "تم التطوير بواسطة ديف ليدو" at the bottom of the page. Considering its placement, I'm now fine-tuning the styling, using Tailwind classes for a consistent look. I'm focusing on ensuring it aligns with the color scheme and general aesthetic, aiming for a discrete, yet visible, addition to the interface.

entries.description: Added a footer at the bottom of the main content area with the text "Developed by Dev Lido" in Arabic.

payload.text:
قم بحذف الاشعارات 

لما مستخدم يسال مين المطور 

الاجابة تكون المطور ليدو

**Implementing Functionality Now**

I'm currently focused on removing existing notifications, scrutinizing the `App` class for relevant methods. Also, I am adding a system instruction to answer "Who is the developer?" with "The developer is Lido" (المطور ليدو). I am adding these two functions into the core system logic.


**Defining Developer Identity**

I've been working on integrating the developer information into the system prompts. Specifically, I'm modifying `Chatbot.tsx` and `LiveConversation.tsx` to include "Your developer is Lido (المطور ليدو)" in their respective system instructions.  The goal is to ensure this information is readily available for relevant queries. Also, I've cleared the `initialNotifications` block from `App.tsx`.


**Streamlining System Instructions**

I'm now streamlining the developer information integration. I've removed the initial notification loading in `App.tsx` completely. Also, I'm taking a look at how prompts are built in both `Chatbot.tsx` and `LiveConversation.tsx` to simplify the addition of the developer identity. It looks like they both create their own prompts. Finally, I'm examining `geminiService.ts` to make that method safer.


**Integrating Developer Information**

I'm now focusing on integrating the developer information comprehensively.  I am applying the updated system instructions to `LiveConversation.tsx`. Also, I'm checking and confirming that `constants.ts` defines system prompts for each course, and that updating the combining logic in `Chatbot.tsx` is the correct strategy. I'm ready to apply these changes.

entries.description: Updated system prompt to include developer information (Lido).

I've integrated the bot logic to respond to queries about the developer. The chatbot now answers "The developer is Lido" in both English and Arabic when users ask "Who is the developer?" or similar questions. This addition should enhance the user experience and reduce any misunderstandings about the platform's creation. Now the main focus is removing the notification icon, which seems pretty straightforward.

I'm now refining the chatbot's response related to the developer's identity. I'm verifying that the response, which states "The developer is Lido" (المطور ليدو), is sufficient to address user queries about the platform's creator. I'm also ensuring the prompt in `components/LiveConversation.tsx` and the system instructions are clear on this. Lastly, I'm beginning the process of removing the unnecessary notification feature's UI elements in `components/Sidebar.tsx` and the modal, as requested, starting with `App.tsx`, and I will ensure that usage of `components/NotificationsModal.tsx` is removed as well.


**Commencing Notification Removal**

Okay, the "Lido" developer instruction checks out.  Now I'm completely removing the notifications feature. This includes the icon, logic, and modals. Starting the deletion process right away, as requested, to streamline the interface and improve user focus.

payload.text: Apply the edits shown in the screenshot(s).قم بحذف مستوي تقدم

I'm aiming for a clean and efficient deletion, minimizing any impact on other components. I'm exploring different approaches to ensure the removal aligns with the overall design.

\n\n

I'm focusing on minimizing impact and maintaining visual consistency after this particular bit is removed. I've pinpointed the relevant code segment and am preparing to implement the changes.


**Deciding on Refinement**

\n\n

I'm now determining how to refine the "Progress Level" removal. While the initial focus was on full removal, I'm considering retaining the arrow button for navigation. The code snippet highlights a potential issue: the blue circle and text may be separate from the arrow, requiring targeted deletion. My goal is to streamline the UI, only dropping the progress text and bar, which includes the blue circle. I want to keep the arrow for navigation, as it could be useful. I'm focusing on minimizing code changes while achieving the intended visual simplification.

I'm now zeroing in on removing the "Progress Level" text from the course cards. I've confirmed that the user's intent, based on the screenshot, is to eliminate that specific element. Currently, I'm focusing on the `div` containing the progress bar and the accompanying text label within the `HomePage.tsx` component. The next step involves adjusting the remaining arrow button's layout to maintain visual harmony.


**Deleting Unused Variable**

\n\n

I've identified the specific code section and am preparing to eliminate the unnecessary variable. This is intended to simplify the code and improve its overall efficiency. I'm taking care to ensure this change doesn't introduce any new bugs or conflicts.

entries.description: Removed the progress level text and progress bar from course cards on the home page as requested.

payload.text:
قم بتغير صورة بكار الذكي الموضحة في الصورة الي صورة دي 

قم بتصميم الصورة باستخدام nano banana pro

**Analyzing Image Locations**

I've pinpointed the image's likely location within `CourseHub`, specifically the header or a Bakkar-specific component, as per the user's screenshot. I'm cross-referencing this with the provided file to verify and trace the image's use.


**Investigating Chatbot Icon**

I've discovered the chatbot icon is generated within `Chatbot.tsx`, specifically a glowing sparkle icon. I need to swap this with the user-provided Bakkar image. The instruction "قم بتصميم الصورة باستخدام nano banana pro" implies image generation via Gemini's `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` is the next step.


**Reconciling Image Requests**

I'm now clarifying the image request. While "nano banana pro" points to image generation, the provided image seems intended as the replacement. My role as a code-generating model limits direct image creation. The prompt is ambiguous - is it a request for code that generates the image, or a simple image swap? I need more context to act appropriately.


**Deciding Image Strategy**

I'm evaluating my options. Following the guidelines, image generation is possible via the API, though directly embedding large images as base64 is discouraged. The user's goal seems to modify the application, and a visual element ("Bakkar") is explicitly provided. Since I can't generate a persistent image asset URL now, I'm considering using a placeholder, revert to the sparkles icon, or, interpret the "design" instruction as my tool utilization.


**Deciphering User Intent**

I'm now grappling with the user's intent. The prompt, "Design the image using nano banana pro," is causing confusion. I'm focusing on the core request: swapping the current icon with a provided cartoon character. I can directly modify the code by replacing the `IconSparkles` div with an `img` tag. However, I still can't store the image. I'm considering placeholder options, or a data URI if possible. I'm trying to decipher if "Design the image" refers to a direct image generation request via the API.


**Refining Image Integration**

I'm now refining my approach, considering the constraints of my coding environment. I understand I can't directly create and save an image file. While I could use a placeholder image or even try a base64 string (if small), that's not ideal. The best plan is either finding a public URL for a "Bakkar cartoon" image, or instructing the user to place the provided image at `/bakkar.png` within the app's structure. However, the user's mention of "nano banana pro" (meaning `gemini-3-pro-image-preview`) introduces a twist.  Could the user be asking me to build *image generation* using that model? I'm re-evaluating the user's intent: Are they requesting a direct image swap, or the creation of an image *generation* feature?


**Selecting Image Source**

I've decided on the avatar URL strategy. I'll modify `Chatbot.tsx` to use the `avatar.iran.liara.run` endpoint with "Bakkar" as the username. This offers a consistent, relevant image while avoiding the need for direct image upload or generation, aligning with my capabilities. This simplifies the direct image swap and addresses the immediate user requirement.


**Updating Icon Integration**

Now, I'm integrating the `Bakkar` image. Since the user specifically requested a design update, I'll prioritize replacing the existing sparkle icon in `Chatbot.tsx`. The relevant code snippet is now modified to use the `https://avatar.iran.liara.run/public/boy?username=Bakkar` URL. This should provide a suitable cartoon image. Furthermore, I've identified the use of the `IconSparkles` component also in `components/LiveConversation.tsx`, and I will now apply the same logic to that location to ensure a consistent look. I'm focusing on UI updates, not image generation.


**Refining Icon Strategy**

I'm now revising my approach. I will update `bot.tsx` with the specified Bakkar image URL (`https://avatar.iran.liara.run/public/boy?username=Bakkar`). To address the "design" request, I'll explain my choice of a matching-style placeholder, as generating and saving images is beyond my current capabilities. I'm focusing on immediate user needs, prioritizing the direct image swap and explaining limitations clearly.


**Applying Text Updates**

I'm now focusing on the text changes. The user wants to update the header text in addition to the icon swap. I need to locate where the header text is defined within the application. The screenshot provides the target text: "Bakkar the Smart" and "Your personal assistant in the subject". I will modify the corresponding text elements within the `Chatbot.tsx` file accordingly, alongside the icon replacement, to ensure all the design updates from the provided screenshots are implemented.
